1:
"Brethren, if a man be overtaken in a fault, ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness; considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted." The witnesses note the gentle restoring of the fallen in meekness. On "if a man be overtaken in a fault," the grammatical voices note the case. Barnes and Robertson note Paul addresses the case of "a man overtaken (caught, surprised, taken unawares) in a fault (a trespass, a sin)" — one who has fallen, perhaps by surprise of temptation rather than deliberate purpose.

On "ye which are spiritual, restore such an one in the spirit of meekness," the witnesses note the duty. Barnes and Calvin note the charge to the "spiritual" (the mature, Spirit-led believers): "restore (katartizo — mend, set right, as a dislocated limb or a torn net) such an one in the spirit of meekness (gentleness)" — the fallen is to be tenderly recovered, not harshly condemned.

On "considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted," the eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the caution: the restorer is to consider himself — his own weakness and liability to fall — and so deal gently, knowing he too could be tempted. The verse displays the gentle restoring of the fallen in meekness.

The witnesses agree: Paul addresses "a man overtaken (caught, surprised) in a fault," one fallen by surprise of temptation (Barnes, Robertson); the charge to the "spiritual" (mature, Spirit-led) to "restore (mend, set right) such an one in the spirit of meekness," tenderly recovering not harshly condemning (Barnes, Calvin); and "considering thyself, lest thou also be tempted," the restorer mindful of his own weakness, dealing gently (Chrysostom, Henry). The verse displays restoring the fallen. The verse marks the case: overtaken in a fault. The verse displays the duty: restore in meekness. The verse displays the restorers: the spiritual. The verse displays the caution: lest thou be tempted. The duty displays the fallen gently recovered. The verse displays the fault: a surprise. The verse displays the restoring: in meekness. The verse displays the self-watch: of the restorer. The gentle restoring of the fallen in meekness displays the law of Christ in practice: when a brother is caught in a fault, the spiritual are to mend him tenderly, in a spirit of gentleness — each mindful of his own weakness and liability to fall, lest he too be tempted. The verse displays the meek restoring of the fallen, fulfilled in bearing burdens (v.2).
2:
"Bear ye one another's burdens, and so fulfil the law of Christ." The witnesses note the call to bear one another's burdens and fulfil Christ's law. On "bear ye one another's burdens," the grammatical voices note the duty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's charge: "bear (carry, help to support) one another's burdens (bare — the heavy loads, the trials, sorrows, infirmities, and even faults that weigh believers down)" — the strong helping the weak, each sharing the other's load.

On "so fulfil the law of Christ," the witnesses note the fulfilment. Barnes and Calvin note that to bear one another's burdens is to "fulfil the law of Christ" — the law of love (John 13:34), the new commandment; Christ's whole law is summed up in this mutual, self-giving love.

The lutheran Luther notes the contrast: over against the law of Moses, which the Judaizers pressed, stands "the law of Christ" — love bearing the brother's burden; this, not circumcision, is the true fulfilling of the law. The verse displays the call to bear one another's burdens and fulfil Christ's law.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's charge "bear (carry, help support) one another's burdens (barē — the heavy loads, trials, sorrows, infirmities, and faults that weigh believers down)," the strong helping the weak (Barnes, Robertson); to do so is to "fulfil the law of Christ" — the law of love (John 13:34), Christ's whole law summed in mutual self-giving love (Barnes, Calvin); this, not circumcision, the true fulfilling of the law (Luther). The verse displays bearing burdens. The verse marks the duty: bear one another's burdens. The verse displays the loads: trials and faults. The verse displays the fulfilment: the law of Christ. The verse displays the contrast: with Moses' law. The call displays the strong bearing the weak's burdens in love. The verse displays the burdens: shared. The verse displays the law: of Christ. The verse displays the love: fulfilling it. The call to bear one another's burdens and fulfil Christ's law displays the heart of Christian community: believers are to help carry each other's heavy loads — trials, sorrows, and even faults — and in this mutual, self-giving love they fulfil the law of Christ, which is love. The verse displays the burden-bearing law of love, guarded against self-conceit (v.3).
3:
"For if a man think himself to be something, when he is nothing, he deceiveth himself." The brief verse notes the warning against self-conceit. The grammatical voices note the folly. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's caution against the pride that hinders burden-bearing: "if a man think himself to be something (important, superior, above helping others) when he is nothing, he deceiveth (deludes) himself" — the conceited man is self-deceived.

On "when he is nothing," the witnesses note the truth. Barnes and Calvin note the reality: in himself, before God, the man is "nothing" — without grace he has no worth or merit; his high opinion of himself is a delusion.

The lutheran Luther notes the connection: pride and self-conceit are what keep men from the lowly work of bearing others' burdens; the proud man, thinking himself above his brethren, deceives himself and fails in love. The verse displays the warning against self-conceit.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's caution against the pride that hinders burden-bearing — "if a man think himself to be something (superior, above helping others) when he is nothing, he deceiveth (deludes) himself" (Barnes, Robertson); in himself before God the man is "nothing," without grace having no worth, his high self-opinion a delusion (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes pride keeps men from the lowly work of bearing burdens. The verse displays the warning against conceit. The verse marks the conceit: thinking himself something. The verse displays the truth: he is nothing. The verse displays the delusion: self-deceived. The verse displays the connection: pride hinders love. The warning displays self-conceit as self-deception. The verse displays the something: imagined. The verse displays the nothing: the truth. The verse displays the deceit: of self. The warning against self-conceit displays the pride that hinders burden-bearing: the man who thinks himself something — too important to stoop and help his brethren — when before God he is nothing, only deceives himself, and fails in the love that bears others' burdens. The verse displays the folly of pride, answered by honest self-examination (vv.4-5). The connection with the preceding verse is close and pointed, for it is precisely the man who overrates his own importance who will not stoop to bear the burden of a brother, imagining himself too lofty for such lowly service; and so the apostle lays bare the secret root of the failure of love, which is the empty self-importance that puffs a man up while leaving him, in the sight of God, exactly nothing.
4:
"But let every man prove his own work, and then shall he have rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another." The brief verse notes the call to test one's own work, not compare with others. The grammatical voices note the self-examination. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's direction against conceited comparison: "let every man prove (test, examine, as assaying metal) his own work" — judge his own conduct by the proper, absolute standard, not by comparison with others.

On "rejoicing in himself alone, and not in another," the witnesses note the ground of glorying. Barnes and Calvin note that then his "rejoicing (ground of boasting)" will be "in himself alone (with reference to his own actual character) and not in another" — not in feeling superior to a worse neighbour, but in the honest worth of his own work before God.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley notes the principle: true ground for rejoicing is found only in an approving conscience, the honest discharge of one's own duty — never in the false comfort of comparing oneself favourably with others. The verse displays the call to test one's own work, not compare with others.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's direction against conceited comparison — "let every man prove (test, examine) his own work," by the proper absolute standard not by comparison (Barnes, Robertson); then his "rejoicing (ground of boasting)" will be "in himself alone" (his own actual worth before God) "and not in another" (not in feeling superior to a worse neighbour, Barnes, Calvin). Wesley notes true ground for rejoicing is an approving conscience, not comparison. The verse displays self-examination. The verse marks the test: prove his own work. The verse displays the standard: absolute, not comparative. The verse displays the rejoicing: in himself alone. The verse displays the warning: not in another. The call displays each judging his own work, not comparing. The verse displays the proving: of one's work. The verse displays the rejoicing: honest. The verse displays the comparison: rejected. The call to test one's own work, not compare with others, displays the cure for self-conceit: each is to examine his own conduct by the proper standard, so that his ground for rejoicing lies in the honest worth of his own work before God — never in the false comfort of measuring himself against a worse neighbour. The verse displays honest self-examination, for each bears his own load (v.5).
5:
"For every man shall bear his own burden." The witnesses note that each must bear his own load before God — resolving the apparent contradiction with v.2. On "every man shall bear his own burden," the grammatical voices note the word and sense. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the key distinction: the "burden (phortion — a load, as a soldier's pack or a ship's cargo)" here is different from the "burdens (barē)" of v.2; this is the load of personal responsibility — each must answer to God for his own actions, a responsibility that cannot be shifted onto another.

On the apparent contradiction, the witnesses resolve it. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note there is no contradiction with v.2 ("bear ye one another's burdens"): there, believers are to share each other's trials and infirmities (barē); here, each must bear alone the phortion of his own accountability before God's judgment — sympathy in trials, but no transfer of responsibility.

The lutheran Luther notes the solemnity: at the last judgment each will stand alone and bear his own burden; the praises of others will not help, but the witness of his own conscience will stand for or against him. The verse displays that each must bear his own load before God.

The witnesses agree the resolution of the apparent contradiction: the "burden (phortion — load of personal responsibility)" of v.5 differs from the "burdens (barē — trials, infirmities)" of v.2; there is no contradiction, for believers are to share one another's trials (v.2) yet each must answer to God alone for his own actions (v.5) — sympathy in trials, but no transfer of accountability (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Luther notes each will stand alone at the judgment, his own conscience witnessing. The verse displays each bearing his own load. The verse marks the word: phortion, not barē. The verse displays the sense: personal accountability. The verse displays the resolution: no contradiction with v.2. The verse displays the judgment: each alone. The load displays the accountability none can shift. The verse displays the burden: his own. The verse displays the bearing: alone. The verse displays the judgment: personal. That each must bear his own load before God displays the resolution of the apparent contradiction with v.2: though believers are to share one another's trials and infirmities (the barē of v.2), each must still bear alone the load (phortion) of his own accountability before God — sympathy in suffering, but no transfer of personal responsibility. The verse displays the solemn individuality of judgment, with the duty of supporting teachers (v.6).
6:
"Let him that is taught in the word communicate unto him that teacheth in all good things." The brief verse notes the duty to support those who teach the word. The grammatical voices note the charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's direction: "let him that is taught (the learner, the catechized) in the word communicate (share, give a share of his good things) unto him that teacheth (the teacher, the minister) in all good things" — the taught are to provide for the temporal support of their teachers.

On "communicate... in all good things," the witnesses note the substance. Barnes and Calvin note that "all good things" means especially material support — the people are to share their temporal goods with those who minister to them in spiritual things (as in 1 Corinthians 9:11); the laborer is worthy of his support.

The puritan Henry and lutheran Luther note the principle: it is the duty and privilege of those who receive spiritual instruction to maintain their teachers; ingratitude toward faithful ministers is a fault. The verse displays the duty to support those who teach the word.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's direction — "let him that is taught (the learner) in the word communicate (share) unto him that teacheth (the minister) in all good things," the taught providing temporal support for their teachers (Barnes, Robertson); "all good things" meaning especially material support, sharing temporal goods with those who minister spiritual things (1 Corinthians 9:11, Barnes, Calvin). Henry and Luther note it is the people's duty to maintain their teachers. The verse displays supporting teachers. The verse marks the duty: communicate to the teacher. The verse displays the taught: the learner. The verse displays the substance: all good things. The verse displays the support: material. The duty displays the taught maintaining their teachers. The verse displays the teaching: received. The verse displays the sharing: of goods. The verse displays the teacher: supported. The duty to support those who teach the word displays a practical obligation of Christian fellowship: those who are taught in the word are to share their good things — especially temporal support — with those who teach them, that faithful ministers may be maintained. The verse displays the support of teachers, grounded in the law of sowing and reaping (vv.7-8).
7:
"Be not deceived; God is not mocked: for whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap." The witnesses note the law of sowing and reaping under God's justice. On "be not deceived; God is not mocked," the grammatical voices note the warning. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's solemn caution: "be not deceived (do not delude yourselves); God is not mocked (mukterizo — cannot be sneered at, outwitted, or evaded)" — none can cheat God or escape his justice by pretence.

On "whatsoever a man soweth, that shall he also reap," the witnesses note the principle. Barnes and Calvin note the fixed law: "whatsoever a man soweth (the kind of seed, the course of life he pursues), that shall he also reap (the corresponding harvest)" — the harvest answers infallibly to the sowing; conduct has consequences God ensures.

The lutheran Luther notes the application: this applies to the support of teachers and to all conduct — those who sow stinginess or fleshly indulgence reap accordingly; God's justice cannot be evaded by hypocrisy. The verse displays the law of sowing and reaping under God's justice.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's solemn caution — "be not deceived; God is not mocked (cannot be outwitted or evaded)," none cheating God or escaping his justice (Barnes, Robertson); the fixed law "whatsoever a man soweth (the course of life he pursues), that shall he also reap (the corresponding harvest)," the harvest answering infallibly to the sowing (Barnes, Calvin). Luther notes God's justice cannot be evaded by hypocrisy. The verse displays sowing and reaping. The verse marks the warning: God is not mocked. The verse displays the law: sow and reap. The verse displays the justice: inescapable. The verse displays the consequence: harvest answers sowing. The law displays God's justice ensuring the harvest. The verse displays the deceit: forbidden. The verse displays the sowing: of conduct. The verse displays the reaping: in kind. The law of sowing and reaping under God's justice displays the inescapable consequence of conduct: God cannot be mocked or evaded, and whatever a man sows — the course of life he pursues — that he will surely reap, the harvest answering infallibly to the seed. The verse displays the certainty of God's justice, applied to flesh and Spirit (v.8).
8:
"For he that soweth to his flesh shall of the flesh reap corruption; but he that soweth to the Spirit shall of the Spirit reap life everlasting." The witnesses note the two sowings and their two harvests. Gill notes that sowing to the flesh is not the lawful care of the body, but pampering and gratifying its lusts, minding the things of the flesh; while sowing to the Spirit is acting under his influence and direction. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he who sows to his flesh shall of the flesh reap corruption, but he who sows to the Spirit shall of the Spirit reap life everlasting; the eternal contrast of the two harvests — that Paul applies the law of sowing to the great choice of life: to live for the flesh (indulging its desires) yields only corruption (ruin and death), while to live by the Spirit (in his ways and obedience) yields the harvest of everlasting life, the two seeds bearing fruit each after its own kind into eternity; soweth to the Spirit... life everlasting. The witnesses note he that soweth to his flesh shall... reap corruption. The witnesses note he that soweth to the Spirit shall... reap life everlasting.

The witnesses note the two sowings — to the flesh, reaping corruption; to the Spirit, reaping life everlasting — the harvest each after its own kind into eternity.

So the witnesses make the verse the two sowings and harvests. The lesson the expositors draw is the eternal contrast: the flesh yields corruption, the Spirit life everlasting. The witnesses note the sowing to flesh: "he that soweth to his flesh shall of the flesh reap corruption" — Gill notes sowing to the flesh is gratifying its lusts, reaping corruption; the sowing to flesh; the gratifying; the lusts; the corruption; the ruin. The expositors observe the corruption: the harvest of the flesh is corruption (ruin, death); the corruption; the harvest; the ruin; the death; the flesh. The witnesses press the sowing to Spirit: "he that soweth to the Spirit shall of the Spirit reap life everlasting" — sowing to the Spirit reaps life everlasting; the sowing to Spirit; the his influence; the obedience; the life everlasting; the harvest. The expositors draw the lesson on the eternal contrast: to live for the flesh yields corruption, to live by the Spirit yields everlasting life; the eternal contrast; the for the flesh; the by the Spirit; the corruption and life; the choice. The witnesses observe the each after its kind: the two seeds bear fruit each after its own kind into eternity; the each after its kind; the the two seeds; the fruit; the own kind; the eternity. The expositors note the great choice: the law of sowing applies to the great choice of life; the great choice; the the law of sowing; the of life; the the seeds; the harvest. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the he that soweth to his flesh shall reap corruption, the he that soweth to the Spirit shall reap life everlasting, and the two harvests. The verse thus records the two sowings and harvests — teaching the eternal contrast: the flesh yields corruption, the Spirit life everlasting.
9:
"And let us not be weary in well doing: for in due season we shall reap, if we faint not." The witnesses note the encouragement to persevere in well doing. Gill notes "well doing" may mean good works of every sort (done according to God's will, from love, in faith, in Christ's strength, for God's glory), or acts of beneficence to Christ's ministers and poor in particular. The witnesses note Paul exhorting them not to be weary in well doing, for in due season they shall reap if they faint not; the promise of a harvest to the persevering — that Paul, lest the toil of doing good discourage, urges patient continuance, with the cheering assurance that the harvest is certain and will come in God's appointed time, so that the only danger is in fainting and giving up before the reaping; if we faint not, we shall reap. The witnesses note let us not be weary in well doing. The witnesses note in due season we shall reap, if we faint not.

The witnesses note the call not to be weary in well doing — the harvest is certain in due season, the only danger being to faint and give up before reaping.

So the witnesses make the verse the encouragement to persevere in well doing. The lesson the expositors draw is the promise of a certain harvest to the persevering, the danger lying in fainting. The witnesses note the not weary: "let us not be weary in well doing" — Gill notes well doing (good works, or beneficence); not to be weary in it; the not weary; the well doing; the good works; the beneficence; the persevere. The expositors observe the due season: "for in due season we shall reap" — in due season (God's appointed time) they shall reap; the due season; the appointed time; the reap; the harvest; the certain. The witnesses press the if we faint not: "if we faint not" — if they do not faint (give up); the if we faint not; the not give up; the persevere; the endure; the continue. The expositors draw the lesson on the certain harvest: the harvest is certain and will come in God's time; the certain harvest; the certain; the God's time; the reap; the promise. The witnesses observe the danger of fainting: the only danger is in fainting and giving up before the reaping; the danger; the fainting; the giving up; the before reaping; the discouragement. The expositors note the patient continuance: Paul urges patient continuance in well doing; the patient continuance; the urges; the well doing; the persevere; the endure. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the let us not be weary in well doing, the in due season we shall reap if we faint not, and the encouragement to persevere. The verse thus records the encouragement to persevere in well doing — teaching the promise of a certain harvest to the persevering, the danger lying in fainting.
10:
"As we have therefore opportunity, let us do good unto all men, especially unto them who are of the household of faith." The witnesses note the scope and priority of doing good. Gill notes "as we have opportunity" means as occasion requires, as objects offer, and as there is ability, while the time of life lasts, which is the time for sowing. Barnes notes this is the true rule about doing good. The witnesses note Paul directing them to do good to all men as they have opportunity, especially to those of the household of faith; the wide reach and special priority of Christian benevolence — that Paul sets the bounds of doing good as wide as opportunity and ability allow, reaching out to all men whatever their state, yet marks a special claim and priority for fellow-believers (the household of faith), who are to be the first and chief objects of the church's love and help; do good unto all men, especially the household of faith. The witnesses note as we have therefore opportunity, let us do good unto all men. The witnesses note especially unto them who are of the household of faith.

The witnesses note the scope of doing good — to all men as opportunity allows, yet especially to the household of faith, who have a special claim on Christian love.

So the witnesses make the verse the scope and priority of doing good. The lesson the expositors draw is the wide reach of benevolence to all men, with special priority for fellow-believers. The witnesses note the opportunity: "as we have therefore opportunity" — Gill notes as occasion and ability allow, while life lasts; the opportunity; the occasion; the ability; the while life lasts; the time. The expositors observe the all men: "let us do good unto all men" — do good to all men, whatever their state; the all men; the good; the whatever their state; the wide; the reach. The witnesses press the household of faith: "especially unto them who are of the household of faith" — especially to fellow-believers; the household of faith; the especially; the fellow-believers; the special; the priority. The expositors draw the lesson on the wide reach: the bounds of doing good are as wide as opportunity and ability; the wide reach; the bounds; the opportunity; the ability; the all men. The witnesses observe the special claim: fellow-believers have a special claim and priority on the church's love; the special claim; the priority; the fellow-believers; the love; the help. The expositors note the first and chief: the household of faith are the first and chief objects of the church's help; the first and chief; the household of faith; the objects; the help; the priority. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as we have opportunity let us do good unto all men, the especially unto them who are of the household of faith, and the scope and priority. The verse thus records the scope and priority of doing good — teaching the wide reach of benevolence to all men, with special priority for fellow-believers.
11:
"Ye see how large a letter I have written unto you with mine own hand." The witnesses note Paul's writing the letter with his own hand. Gill notes the phrase may mean "how large a letter" (the length) or "with what letters" (the size of the characters), which some understand of the largeness of the characters he wrote, others of his unpracticed hand, being a Hebrew. The witnesses note Paul calling attention to the large letter (or large characters) he had written with his own hand; the mark of personal love and earnestness — that Paul, who often dictated and signed only the close, here points out that he wrote this letter (or much of it) with his own hand, the unusual labour being a token of his deep love for the Galatians and his earnest concern for the weighty matter, that they might weigh the more what cost him such pains; how large a letter... with mine own hand. The witnesses note how large a letter. The witnesses note I have written unto you with mine own hand.

The witnesses note Paul's writing with his own hand — the unusual labour a token of his deep love and earnest concern for the Galatians in this weighty matter.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's writing with his own hand. The lesson the expositors draw is the mark of personal love and earnestness in his unusual labour of writing himself. The witnesses note the large letter: "how large a letter" — Gill notes this may mean the length or the size of the characters; the large letter; the length; the characters; the size; the the writing. The expositors observe the own hand: "I have written unto you with mine own hand" — Paul wrote with his own hand; the own hand; the wrote himself; the personal; the labour; the hand. The witnesses press the unusual labour: Paul, who often dictated, here writes himself — an unusual labour; the unusual labour; the dictated; the himself; the the pains; the effort. The expositors draw the lesson on the token of love: the labour was a token of his deep love for the Galatians; the token of love; the deep love; the the Galatians; the labour; the affection. The witnesses observe the earnest concern: the writing showed his earnest concern for the weighty matter; the earnest concern; the weighty matter; the concern; the the matter; the earnest. The expositors note the weigh the more: they might weigh the more what cost him such pains; the weigh the more; the cost; the pains; the the matter; the heed. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the how large a letter, the I have written unto you with mine own hand, and the mark of love. The verse thus records Paul's writing with his own hand — teaching the mark of personal love and earnestness in his unusual labour.
12:
"As many as desire to make a fair shew in the flesh, they constrain you to be circumcised; only lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ." The witnesses note the false teachers' selfish motive in urging circumcision. Gill notes the false apostles desired to make a fair show outwardly, to appear righteous before men and gain their applause; this exposes their views, aims, and ends. The witnesses note Paul exposing that those who desire to make a fair show in the flesh constrain them to be circumcised, only to escape persecution for the cross of Christ; the cowardice and self-interest behind the false teaching — that Paul lays bare the real motive of the Judaizers: not zeal for God, but a craving for outward show and the desire to avoid the persecution that faithful preaching of the cross brings, for by pressing circumcision on the Gentiles they kept in with the Jews and shielded themselves from the offence of the cross; lest they should suffer persecution for the cross. The witnesses note desire to make a fair shew in the flesh. The witnesses note lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ.

The witnesses note the false teachers' motive — to make a fair show and escape persecution for the cross — exposing their cowardice and self-interest.

So the witnesses make the verse the false teachers' selfish motive. The lesson the expositors draw is the cowardice and self-interest behind the false teaching, avoiding the persecution of the cross. The witnesses note the fair show: "as many as desire to make a fair shew in the flesh" — Gill notes they desired to appear righteous before men; the fair show; the in the flesh; the outwardly; the applause; the appear. The expositors observe the constraining: "they constrain you to be circumcised" — they constrain the Gentiles to be circumcised; the constraining; the circumcised; the press; the the Gentiles; the urge. The witnesses press the motive: "only lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ" — only to escape persecution for the cross; the motive; the escape persecution; the the cross; the avoid; the self-interest. The expositors draw the lesson on the cowardice: the real motive was cowardice — avoiding the persecution faithful preaching brings; the cowardice; the avoiding; the persecution; the faithful preaching; the fear. The witnesses observe the keeping in with the Jews: by pressing circumcision they kept in with the Jews and shielded themselves; the keeping in; the the Jews; the shielded; the pressing circumcision; the self-interest. The expositors note the offence of the cross: they avoided the offence of the cross; the offence of the cross; the avoided; the the cross; the shielded; the escape. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the desire to make a fair shew in the flesh, the lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ, and the selfish motive. The verse thus records the false teachers' selfish motive — teaching the cowardice and self-interest behind the false teaching.
13:
"For neither they themselves who are circumcised keep the law; but desire to have you circumcised, that they may glory in your flesh." The witnesses note the hypocrisy and vainglory of the circumcision party. Gill notes the pleaders for circumcision (whether themselves circumcised or not) did not keep the law themselves. Barnes notes it was true in general that they did not keep it. The witnesses note Paul declaring that even the circumcised do not keep the law themselves, yet desire to have the Galatians circumcised that they may glory in their flesh; the inconsistency and self-glorying of the Judaizers — that Paul exposes a double fault in the false teachers: they who pressed the law on others did not keep it themselves (proving their insincerity), and their real aim in winning Gentile converts to circumcision was not the Galatians' good but their own boasting, that they might glory in the number they had made proselytes; that they may glory in your flesh. The witnesses note neither they themselves who are circumcised keep the law. The witnesses note that they may glory in your flesh.

The witnesses note the Judaizers' hypocrisy and vainglory — they keep not the law themselves, yet press circumcision to glory in the Galatians' flesh.

So the witnesses make the verse the hypocrisy and vainglory of the circumcision party. The lesson the expositors draw is the double fault of the false teachers — not keeping the law themselves, and seeking their own boasting. The witnesses note the not keeping: "neither they themselves who are circumcised keep the law" — Gill notes they did not keep the law themselves; the not keeping; the the law; the themselves; the insincere; the hypocrisy. The expositors observe the insincerity: their not keeping the law proved their insincerity; the insincerity; the not keeping; the proved; the hypocrisy; the false. The witnesses press the desire: "but desire to have you circumcised" — they desire the Galatians circumcised; the desire; the circumcised; the the Galatians; the win; the press. The expositors draw the lesson on the self-glorying: "that they may glory in your flesh" — their aim was to glory in the Galatians' flesh (their proselytes); the self-glorying; the glory; the in your flesh; the proselytes; the boasting. The witnesses observe the not the Galatians' good: their aim was not the Galatians' good but their own boasting; the not the Galatians' good; the own boasting; the their aim; the self; the vainglory. The expositors note the number of proselytes: they would glory in the number they had made proselytes; the number; the proselytes; the made; the glory; the boasting. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither they themselves who are circumcised keep the law, the that they may glory in your flesh, and the hypocrisy and vainglory. The verse thus records the hypocrisy and vainglory of the circumcision party — teaching the double fault of the false teachers, not keeping the law and seeking their own boasting.
14:
"But God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ, by whom the world is crucified unto me, and I unto the world." The witnesses note Paul's glorying in the cross alone. Gill notes Paul expresses his aversion to glorying in anything these men did — not in his Jewish privileges, his legal righteousness, his gifts, or his labours, but in the cross of Christ. The witnesses note Paul declaring that he will glory in nothing but the cross of the Lord Jesus, by which the world is crucified to him and he to the world; the cross as the only boast and the great divorce from the world — that Paul, against the Judaizers' glorying in the flesh, makes the crucified Christ his one and only boast, and testifies that through that cross a mutual death has come between him and the world: the world (its honours, pleasures, and righteousness) is dead and abhorrent to him as a crucified thing, and he is dead to the world, its attractions and threats no longer holding him; God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross. The witnesses note God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross. The witnesses note the world is crucified unto me, and I unto the world.

The witnesses note Paul glorying in the cross alone — by which the world is crucified to him and he to the world; the cross his only boast and his divorce from the world.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's glorying in the cross alone. The lesson the expositors draw is the cross as Paul's only boast and the mutual death it works between him and the world. The witnesses note the only glory: "God forbid that I should glory, save in the cross of our Lord Jesus Christ" — Gill notes Paul glories in nothing but the cross; the only glory; the save in the cross; the nothing else; the the cross; the boast. The expositors observe the not the flesh: not in Jewish privileges, legal righteousness, gifts, or labours; the not the flesh; the not privileges; the not righteousness; the not labours; the only the cross. The witnesses press the world crucified: "by whom the world is crucified unto me" — through the cross the world is crucified to him; the world crucified; the to me; the dead; the abhorrent; the crucified thing. The expositors draw the lesson on the mutual death: "and I unto the world" — and he is crucified to the world; a mutual death; the mutual death; the I to the world; the dead; the both; the divorce. The witnesses observe the world dead to him: the world (its honours, pleasures, righteousness) is dead and abhorrent to him; the world dead; the honours; the pleasures; the righteousness; the abhorrent. The expositors note the dead to the world: he is dead to the world, its attractions and threats no longer holding him; the dead to the world; the attractions; the threats; the no longer; the free. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the God forbid that I should glory save in the cross, the the world is crucified unto me and I unto the world, and the cross the only boast. The verse thus records Paul's glorying in the cross alone — teaching the cross as Paul's only boast and the mutual death it works between him and the world.
15:
"For in Christ Jesus neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision, but a new creature." The witnesses note that only the new creation avails in Christ. Gill notes this gives a reason why Paul gloried in a crucified Christ and counted the world dead to him — particularly the worldly sanctuary of the Jews and all its rites; circumcision and uncircumcision being of no account. The witnesses note Paul declaring that in Christ neither circumcision nor uncircumcision avails anything, but a new creature; the new creation as the one thing that counts — that Paul, repeating the principle, sweeps aside the whole circumcision dispute as worthless in Christ, where outward marks count for nothing, and names the one thing that truly avails: a "new creature" (a new creation), the inward, regenerating work of the Spirit that makes a man over in Christ, which alone marks the true child of God; a new creature. The witnesses note neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision. The witnesses note but a new creature.

The witnesses note that in Christ neither circumcision nor uncircumcision avails, but a new creature — the inward, regenerating work that alone marks the true child of God.

So the witnesses make the verse the new creation as the one thing that avails. The lesson the expositors draw is that outward marks count for nothing in Christ, but the new creature, the Spirit's regenerating work. The witnesses note the neither: "neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision" — Gill notes circumcision and uncircumcision are of no account; the neither; the circumcision; the uncircumcision; the no account; the worthless. The expositors observe the in Christ: "in Christ Jesus" — in Christ, the outward marks count nothing; the in Christ; the outward marks; the nothing; the worthless; the dispute. The witnesses press the new creature: "but a new creature" — but a new creature (new creation); the new creature; the new creation; the the one thing; the avails; the inward. The expositors draw the lesson on the regenerating work: the new creature is the inward, regenerating work of the Spirit; the regenerating work; the inward; the the Spirit; the new creation; the makes over. The witnesses observe the made over in Christ: the new creature is a man made over in Christ; the made over; the in Christ; the renewed; the new; the regenerate. The expositors note the true child: the new creation alone marks the true child of God; the true child; the new creation; the marks; the of God; the regenerate. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the neither circumcision availeth any thing nor uncircumcision, the but a new creature, and the new creation. The verse thus records the new creation as the one thing that avails — teaching that outward marks count for nothing in Christ, but the new creature, the Spirit's regenerating work.
16:
"And as many as walk according to this rule, peace be on them, and mercy, and upon the Israel of God." The witnesses note the blessing on those who walk by the gospel rule. Gill notes the "rule" (or canon) is not the canon of Scripture in general, but the doctrine of justification by Christ's righteousness and the new creation, which Paul had been explaining and confirming. The witnesses note Paul pronouncing peace and mercy on as many as walk according to this rule, and upon the Israel of God; the benediction on the true people of God — that Paul, having laid down the rule (the gospel of the new creation, not circumcision), invokes peace and mercy on all who walk by it, and on "the Israel of God"; the witnesses note the debate on this phrase — whether it adds a special blessing on believing Jews (the true Israel within the nation), or is another name for the whole church (Jew and Gentile) as the spiritual Israel, the true covenant people; peace and mercy upon the Israel of God. The witnesses note as many as walk according to this rule. The witnesses note peace... and upon the Israel of God.

The witnesses note the blessing of peace and mercy on those who walk by the gospel rule, and on "the Israel of God" — with debate over whom that names.

So the witnesses make the verse the benediction on the true people of God. The lesson the expositors draw is the blessing of peace and mercy on all who walk by the gospel rule of the new creation. The witnesses note the rule: "as many as walk according to this rule" — Gill notes the rule is the doctrine of justification and the new creation; the rule; the the doctrine; the justification; the new creation; the canon. The expositors observe the blessing: "peace be on them, and mercy" — peace and mercy on those who walk by it; the blessing; the peace; the mercy; the on them; the benediction. The witnesses present the Israel of God: "and upon the Israel of God" — the witnesses note the debate on this phrase; the Israel of God; the the phrase; the debated; the people; the blessing. The expositors weigh the readings: some take "the Israel of God" as a special blessing on believing Jews (the true Israel within the nation); others as another name for the whole church (Jew and Gentile), the spiritual Israel, the true covenant people; both hold the blessing belongs to the gospel-walkers. The witnesses draw the lesson on the true people: the blessing belongs to the true people of God — those who walk by the gospel rule; the true people; the of God; the gospel rule; the blessing; the peace. The expositors observe the peace and mercy: peace (with God and conscience) and mercy (the fountain of all good) are invoked; the peace and mercy; the with God; the the fountain; the invoked; the blessing. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the as many as walk according to this rule, the peace and upon the Israel of God, and the benediction. The verse thus records the benediction on the true people of God — teaching the blessing of peace and mercy on all who walk by the gospel rule.
17:
"From henceforth let no man trouble me: for I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus." The witnesses note Paul's claim to the marks of the Lord Jesus. Gill notes that, having so fully proved justification is not by works but by faith, and that circumcision and other rituals were not necessary, Paul in an authoritative way requires that they give him no further trouble on that head. The witnesses note Paul charging that henceforth no man trouble him, for he bears in his body the marks of the Lord Jesus; the badge of true apostleship in his scars — that Paul, against the Judaizers who gloried in the mark of circumcision, points to the marks (the brands, stigmata) he bears in his own body — the scars of the beatings, stonings, and sufferings endured for Christ — as the true and only badge he needs, the visible proof that he belongs to and serves the Lord Jesus, before which the troublers must fall silent; I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus. The witnesses note from henceforth let no man trouble me. The witnesses note I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus.

The witnesses note Paul's claim — let no man trouble him, for he bears the marks of the Lord Jesus — his scars of suffering the true badge of his apostleship.

So the witnesses make the verse Paul's claim to the marks of the Lord Jesus. The lesson the expositors draw is the badge of true apostleship in his scars, before which the troublers must fall silent. The witnesses note the charge: "from henceforth let no man trouble me" — Gill notes Paul authoritatively requires no further trouble; the charge; the no man trouble; the authoritative; the no further; the silence. The expositors observe the marks: "I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus" — Paul bears the marks (brands, stigmata) in his body; the marks; the in my body; the brands; the stigmata; the scars. The witnesses press the scars of suffering: the marks are the scars of the beatings, stonings, and sufferings endured for Christ; the scars; the beatings; the stonings; the sufferings; the for Christ. The expositors draw the lesson on the true badge: these scars are the true and only badge Paul needs — against the Judaizers' circumcision; the true badge; the only; the against circumcision; the the Judaizers; the proof. The witnesses observe the proof of belonging: the marks are the visible proof that Paul belongs to and serves the Lord Jesus; the proof of belonging; the visible; the belongs; the serves; the the Lord. The expositors note the troublers silenced: before this proof the troublers must fall silent; the troublers silenced; the fall silent; the the proof; the no further; the authoritative. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the from henceforth let no man trouble me, the I bear in my body the marks of the Lord Jesus, and the badge of apostleship. The verse thus records Paul's claim to the marks of the Lord Jesus — teaching the badge of true apostleship in his scars, before which the troublers must fall silent.
18:
"Brethren, the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen." The witnesses note Paul's closing benediction of grace. Gill notes Paul calls them "brethren" to testify his affection, notwithstanding their instability and the roughness with which he had treated them, and to show his humility in owning the relation, which the pride of the false teachers would not allow. The witnesses note Paul closing the letter with the prayer that the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with their spirit, sealed with "Amen"; the loving close in grace — that Paul, who had dealt sharply with the Galatians for the truth's sake, ends the whole letter as a brother, in tenderness, invoking on them the very grace of Christ he had been defending, that it might be with their inmost spirit, a fitting and gracious seal to an epistle of grace against the law; the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. The witnesses note brethren. The witnesses note the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit. Amen.

The witnesses note Paul's closing benediction — the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ with their spirit — ending as a brother, in tenderness, with the grace he defended.

So the witnesses make the verse the closing benediction of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is the loving close, Paul ending as a brother with the very grace of Christ he had defended. The witnesses note the brethren: "brethren" — Gill notes Paul calls them brethren, testifying affection despite his sharpness; the brethren; the affection; the despite sharpness; the tender; the relation. The expositors observe the humility: Paul's owning the relation shows his humility, unlike the proud false teachers; the humility; the owning; the relation; the unlike the proud; the lowly. The witnesses press the grace: "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit" — the prayer that Christ's grace be with their spirit; the grace; the of Christ; the with your spirit; the prayer; the benediction. The expositors draw the lesson on the loving close: Paul, who dealt sharply for the truth, ends in tenderness; the loving close; the dealt sharply; the for the truth; the tenderness; the brother. The witnesses observe the grace defended: he invokes the very grace of Christ he had been defending; the grace defended; the invokes; the Christ's grace; the defended; the the gospel. The expositors note the fitting seal: the benediction is a fitting and gracious seal to an epistle of grace against the law; the fitting seal; the gracious; the epistle of grace; the against the law; the close. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the brethren, the the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with your spirit Amen, and the closing benediction. The verse thus records the closing benediction of grace — teaching the loving close, Paul ending as a brother with the very grace of Christ he had defended.
